source: All-In Podcast — 2025 predictions (bestie guest Gavin Baker)
summary: Traditional auto OEMs are uninvestable and will be forced into mega-mergers as Tesla and Chinese EVs dominate
summary_extended: Baker fully endorses Chamath's thesis that traditional auto OEMs — particularly European and Japanese manufacturers — are structurally uninvestable, caught in a vice between Tesla's software and autonomy advantage on the high end and Chinese EV manufacturers' overwhelming cost advantage on the low end. He argues these OEMs are losing their Chinese business because they no longer make competitive products, and that the Honda-Nissan merger is a leading indicator of the forced consolidation that will follow as these companies fight for survival. Baker implies a short or underweight positioning on traditional OEMs, though he declines to discuss specific positions directly. The primary risk he identifies is government intervention, since OEMs are national champions and major employers, which could slow but not reverse the structural decline. No specific price targets or timeframes are given, but the framing is medium-term and high-conviction.
detail: Baker agrees with Chamath's thesis that European and Japanese OEMs are melting icebergs caught between Tesla's software/autonomy advantage and Chinese OEMs' cost advantage. Honda-Nissan merger is a leading indicator. The only risk to this thesis is government intervention given OEMs are national champions and large employers. Baker implies short positioning.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I agree 100% with chamath' and implies personal positioning ('I would rather not talk about specific positions')
quote_fragment: they're going to lose their Chinese business because they don't make competitive products anymore

ticker_audit.reasoning: Stellantis is a major European/American OEM directly subject to the structural decline thesis Baker is describing — caught between Tesla and Chinese EVs.
Volkswagen is a leading European OEM and a primary example of the melting iceberg thesis; already facing severe financial stress and restructuring.
Honda is explicitly named via the Honda-Nissan merger as a leading indicator of forced OEM consolidation, making it a direct subject of this thesis.
Nissan is the other party in the Honda-Nissan merger Baker cites as a leading indicator; directly subject to the structural decline thesis.

Ford is a major traditional OEM facing the same structural pressures from Tesla and Chinese EVs described in the thesis. [RE-AUDIT: Baker's thesis focuses on European and Japanese OEMs. Ford is a US OEM and there is no specific mention of Ford in connection with this thesis. The thesis summary notes Baker 'implies' a short on traditional OEMs broadly, but Ford was not specifically named or connected to this exact claim.]

GM is a major traditional OEM subject to the same structural squeeze; Baker's thesis applies broadly to legacy auto manufacturers including US ones. [RE-AUDIT: Same reasoning as Ford — Baker's thesis specifically calls out European and Japanese manufacturers. GM was not specifically named or connected to this exact thesis. Applying the thesis broadly to all legacy OEMs including US ones goes beyond what Baker specifically said.]
Baker explicitly names Tesla as one of the two forces (software/autonomy advantage) squeezing traditional OEMs, making Tesla a clear and unambiguous direct beneficiary of this structural shift.
